title: Work
text: A showcase of projects focused on environmental science, public-sector data systems, and reproducible analysis.

title: Work
text:
Relevant Project Experience

Our Freshwater 2026 - Internal Consistency Checking — Ministry for the Environment publication support; Environmental reporting, QA, data assurance

Contributed to internal consistency checking of Our Freshwater 2026 report before publication.
Supported quality assurance of environmental evidence used to inform national and regional policy discussions.
Link: https://environment.govt.nz/publications/our-freshwater-2026/

National Indicators - River and Lake Water Quality — Stats NZ Environmental Reporting team collaboration; R, indicator data, publication workflows

Collaborated with analysts in the Environmental Reporting team to publish national indicator data for river and lake water quality.
Helped maintain reliable and transparent data pipelines that support environmental decision-making.
Link: https://www.stats.govt.nz/indicators/river-water-quality-nitrogen-data-to-2024/

Ngā Tūtōhu Aotearoa - Indicators Aotearoa New Zealand — Technical lead for environmental and wellbeing indicators platform; R Shiny, data products, stakeholder communication

Led technical development of the Indicators Aotearoa New Zealand platform, integrating diverse environmental datasets into accessible reporting tools.
Worked with internal and external users to translate complex data needs into practical, customer-focused outputs.
Link: https://statisticsnz.shinyapps.io/wellbeingindicators/

Customer-Centred Data Delivery Across Environmental Roles — Stats NZ and Earth Sciences New Zealand (formerly NIWA); Stakeholder engagement, service delivery, communication

Delivered data products and support to a wide range of stakeholders and customers in both public sector and science service settings.
Built strong working relationships through clear communication, responsiveness, and practical problem solving.
Mentored analysts and early career staff across Stats NZ and Earth Sciences New Zealand. Earlier on as a Biology Professor, supporting capability growth, confidence, and collaborative team performance.

title: Work
section: Predicting Macroalgal Introductions at Global and Regional Scales
text:
Predicting Macroalgal Introductions at Global and Regional Scales

Period: Dec 2015 - Nov 2019
Status: Completed
Technologies: LaTeX, R, Git, Machine Learning, GIS
Description: Doctoral thesis focused on predicting the introduction and spread of invasive macroalgal species using machine learning and spatial analysis.

Highlights:

Predictive models using Random Forest and MaxEnt for invasion hotspots.
Analysis of environmental and biological drivers.
Peer-reviewed publications and conference presentations.
Contributions to open-source ecological modeling workflows.

Links:

GitHub

title: Work
text:
Git Learning Environment

Period: August 2024
Status: Current
Technologies: Git, shell scripting, R
Description: A repository to test Git skills through unsupervised challenges, from beginner to expert levels, set in a magical fantasy world.

Highlights:

Immersive storylines and characters for each challenge.
Tasks covering branching, merging, rebasing, and conflict resolution.
Automated feedback and hints for learners.
Open-sourced design for community contributions.

Links:

GitHub

title: Work
text:
Interactive Population Clock (Demo)

Period: September 2025
Status: Current
Technologies: R plumber, REST API, Docker, JavaScript, HTML, CSS
Description: Interactive Population Clock built with Plumber and deployed with Docker.

Highlights:

Real-time population REST API implementation.
Containerized deployment for reproducibility.
Cloud-hosted deployment on Render.
Interactive front-end experience.

Links:

GitHub
Live demo

title: Penguinops
text:
Tēnā koutou!
Ko Nestor Robinson tōku ingoa
He matua ahau
Ko tōku whānau taku pou herenga
I tipu ake ahau ki Mēhiko
E noho ana ahau ki Taratahi
Mōku ake,
Ko te tautoko i ngā tāngata
Ko te waihanga rongoā whai hua
Ānei tāku kaupapa mahi.
Tīhei mauriora!

My name is Nestor Robinson
I am a father
My family is my foundation.
I grew up in Mexico.
I live in Carterton.
For me,
Supporting people,
Creating meaningful solutions,
This is my purpose in work.



A bout Me
I am Nestor M. Robinson, the mind behind Penguinops.
Environmental Scientist | Data Analyst
I turn environmental monitoring data into practical evidence for regional councils, local authorities, and environmental consultancies. With expertise in R, Python, geospatial workflows, and reproducible reporting, I build analysis that supports policy, planning, and on-the-ground decisions.
My programming days began before GenAI and LLMs made everyone look like a genius. I am a self-taught coder who has spent enough hours debugging to qualify for an honorary degree in existential dread. In those days, the best debugging tools were caffeine and denial, sometimes in that order.
Learn More About Me View My Work Explore Indicators

GitHub LinkedIn Email

title: Indicators
text:
This section presents compact environmental indicators for policy and planning audiences, including regional councils, local authorities, and environmental consultancies.


Water Consents Dashboard
A compact view of consented freshwater takes by use type and source, with map context for where takes are located.
Open indicator

Freshwater Consent records Northland focus



Freshwater Habitat Quality
Interactive summary of habitat quality scores, dominant landcover context, and site-level distribution.
Open indicator

Habitat condition Site monitoring Multi-region



Groundwater Quality Trends
Exploration view of long-term groundwater quality data structure and measured variables.
Open indicator

Groundwater quality 1964-2014 state Trend context



River Water Quality Nitrogen Trends
Exploration view of river nitrogen trend records by period, direction, and site geography.
Open indicator

River quality 1991-2020 trends Nitrogen focus









NoteSource and transparency



All pages include a Methods and source section and a collapsed code block so readers can inspect the exact analysis steps.

title: Freshwater Habitat Quality
text: Most monitored sites in this dataset are rated Fair to Good, with a smaller set of Excellent sites often associated with native or less-modified catchments.

title: Freshwater Habitat Quality
text: Most monitored sites in this dataset are rated Fair to Good, with a smaller set of Excellent sites often associated with native or less-modified catchments.

title: Freshwater Habitat Quality
text:
Why this matters for councils
Habitat-quality indicators support prioritization of restoration investment, catchment action plans, and communication of where ecological gains are being retained or lost.

title: Freshwater Habitat Quality
text:
Interactive overview


Code
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(plotly)
library(leaflet)

habitat <- read_csv("../data/freshwater-physical-habitat/habitat_quality_scores.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

by_class <- habitat |>
  count(habitat_quality, sort = TRUE)

plot_ly(
  by_class,
  x = ~reorder(habitat_quality, n),
  y = ~n,
  type = "bar",
  marker = list(color = "#ff7a59")
) |>
  layout(
    xaxis = list(title = "Habitat class"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Number of sites")
  )








Code
plot_ly(
  habitat,
  x = ~dominant_landcover,
  y = ~habitat_quality_score,
  color = ~habitat_quality,
  colors = c("#ef4444", "#f59e0b", "#10b981"),
  type = "box"
) |>
  layout(
    xaxis = list(title = "Dominant landcover"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Habitat quality score")
  )








Code
leaflet(habitat) |>
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) |>
  addCircleMarkers(
    lng = ~long,
    lat = ~lat,
    radius = 4,
    stroke = FALSE,
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    color = "#2b7a78",
    popup = ~paste0(
      "Site: ", site_name,
      "<br>Region: ", region,
      "<br>Score: ", habitat_quality_score,
      "<br>Class: ", habitat_quality,
      "<br>Landcover: ", dominant_landcover
    )
  )

title: Freshwater Habitat Quality
text:
Methods and source

Source: freshwater physical habitat indicator dataset.
File used: data/freshwater-physical-habitat/habitat_quality_scores.csv.
Indicator fields include score, class, landcover context, and site coordinates.
Caveat: this is a compact communication layer; interpretation should include local context and sampling design.







NoteShow code





The complete source code is available in folded code chunks above.

title: A Game Changer in My Data Science Journey: A Year of Self-Hosting with Proxmox VE
text:
It started innocently enough: a dusty ex-lease desktop, a vague memory of someone online saying “Proxmox is awesome”, and a dangerous amount of curiosity.
A year later, my home office looked like a low-budget science-fiction movie.

title: A Game Changer in My Data Science Journey: A Year of Self-Hosting with Proxmox VE
text:
It started innocently enough: a dusty ex-lease desktop, a vague memory of someone online saying “Proxmox is awesome”, and a dangerous amount of curiosity.
A year later, my home office looked like a low-budget science-fiction movie.

title: A Game Changer in My Data Science Journey: A Year of Self-Hosting with Proxmox VE
section: Enter Proxmox: The Rabbit Hole Beckons
text:
Enter Proxmox: The Rabbit Hole Beckons
When I first installed Proxmox VE, I thought, “How hard could it be?”
Within days, evenings were spent spinning up LXC containers and virtual machines like a caffeinated DJ at a tech conference.

title: A Game Changer in My Data Science Journey: A Year of Self-Hosting with Proxmox VE
section: From Hello World to Hello, World of Problems
text:
From Hello World to Hello, World of Problems
Most of my early adventures centered on programming environments, data pipelines, and getting JupyterLab to cooperate. Soon, I was running media servers, exploring distributed databases, and learning hard lessons about networking.

title: A Game Changer in My Data Science Journey: A Year of Self-Hosting with Proxmox VE
section: Lessons Learned (Or: How I Learned to Love Snapshots)
text:
Lessons Learned (Or: How I Learned to Love Snapshots)
Snapshots became my safety net. Before every risky change, I took a snapshot, crossed my fingers, and experimented freely.
Through all the breaking and fixing, Proxmox became a cornerstone of my data science workflow: enabling rapid experiments, safer failures, and faster iteration.

title: A Game Changer in My Data Science Journey: A Year of Self-Hosting with Proxmox VE
text:
Final Thoughts
If you are thinking about building a home lab, do it. You will learn far more than expected about infrastructure, reliability, and your own debugging patience.
May your containers always start, your VMs stay calm, and your family never notice the 2am reboots.

title: Measuring What Matters: Reflections on Nga Tutohu Aotearoa
text: Note: This post reflects my personal views and experiences working on the Nga Tutohu Aotearoa project. It does not represent the official position of any organisation or government agency.

title: Measuring What Matters: Reflections on Nga Tutohu Aotearoa
text:
Introduction
Societal progress is often measured through economic metrics such as GDP, but those numbers are only part of the picture. Working on Nga Tutohu Aotearoa reinforced that meaningful progress must include wellbeing.

title: Measuring What Matters: Reflections on Nga Tutohu Aotearoa
text:
What Is Nga Tutohu Aotearoa?
Nga Tutohu Aotearoa is a national wellbeing framework with indicators across social, environmental, and economic dimensions. It supports policy design that values long-term sustainability and collective wellbeing.
At its core, the framework aims to:

Measure what matters to people and communities
Track wellbeing outcomes over time and across groups
Support evidence-informed policy
Reflect Te Ao Maori perspectives

title: Measuring What Matters: Reflections on Nga Tutohu Aotearoa
text:
My Role and Reflections
My work focused on data integration, dashboard development, and analytical consistency. Through this, I saw how deeply interconnected wellbeing outcomes are across domains.

title: Measuring What Matters: Reflections on Nga Tutohu Aotearoa
text:
From Data to Insight

1. Context Matters
Indicators need lived context. A metric can improve while lived wellbeing declines.


2. Accessibility Drives Impact
Well-designed dashboards and visual tools broaden participation and understanding.


3. Consistency Enables Trust
Metadata quality, version control, and reproducible workflows are essential for credibility.

title: Measuring What Matters: Reflections on Nga Tutohu Aotearoa
text:
Te Ao Maori and Wellbeing
Integrating Te Ao Maori perspectives expanded the analytical lens by emphasizing collective and relational wellbeing, values such as manaakitanga, whanaungatanga, and kaitiakitanga, and the stewardship role of data.

title: Measuring What Matters: Reflections on Nga Tutohu Aotearoa
text:
Conclusion
Data science is not only about managing information; it is also about shaping narratives that influence collective choices. When those narratives reflect people, place, and values, they can drive more inclusive progress.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text:
We as members, contributors, and leaders pledge to make participation in our community a harassment-free experience for everyone, regardless of age, body size, visible or invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity and expression, level of experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, religion, or sexual identity and orientation.
We pledge to act and interact in ways that contribute to an open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, and healthy community.



Examples of behavior that contributes to a positive environment for our community include:

Demonstrating empathy and kindness toward other people
Being respectful of differing opinions, viewpoints, and experiences
Giving and gracefully accepting constructive feedback
Accepting responsibility and apologizing to those affected by our mistakes, and learning from the experience
Focusing on what is best not just for us as individuals, but for the overall community

Examples of unacceptable behavior include:

The use of sexualized language or imagery, and sexual attention or advances of any kind
Trolling, insulting or derogatory comments, and personal or political attacks
Public or private harassment
Publishing others’ private information, such as a physical or email address, without their explicit permission
Other conduct which could reasonably be considered inappropriate in a professional setting




Community leaders are responsible for clarifying and enforcing our standards of acceptable behavior and will take appropriate and fair corrective action in response to any behavior that they deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, or harmful.
Community leaders have the right and responsibility to remove, edit, or reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, and other contributions that are not aligned to this Code of Conduct, and will communicate reasons for moderation decisions when appropriate.



This Code of Conduct applies within all community spaces, and also applies when an individual is officially representing the community in public spaces. Examples of representing our community include using an official e-mail address, posting via an official social media account, or acting as an appointed representative at an online or offline event.



Instances of abusive, harassing, or otherwise unacceptable behavior may be reported to the community leaders responsible for enforcement at . All complaints will be reviewed and investigated promptly and fairly.
All community leaders are obligated to respect the privacy and security of the reporter of any incident.



Community leaders will follow these Community Impact Guidelines in determining the consequences for any action they deem in violation of this Code of Conduct:


Community Impact: Use of inappropriate language or other behavior deemed unprofessional or unwelcome in the community.
Consequence: A private, written warning from community leaders, providing clarity around the nature of the violation and an explanation of why the behavior was inappropriate. A public apology may be requested.



Community Impact: A violation through a single incident or series of actions.
Consequence: A warning with consequences for continued behavior. No interaction with the people involved, including unsolicited interaction with those enforcing the Code of Conduct, for a specified period of time. This includes avoiding interactions in community spaces as well as external channels like social media. Violating these terms may lead to a temporary or permanent ban.



Community Impact: A serious violation of community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior.
Consequence: A temporary ban from any sort of interaction or public communication with the community for a specified period of time. No public or private interaction with the people involved, including unsolicited interaction with those enforcing the Code of Conduct, is allowed during this period. Violating these terms may lead to a permanent ban.



Community Impact: Demonstrating a pattern of violation of community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment of an individual, or aggression toward or disparagement of classes of individuals.
Consequence: A permanent ban from any sort of public interaction within the community.




This Code of Conduct is adapted from the Contributor Covenant, version 2.0, available at https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/0/code_of_conduct.html.
Community Impact Guidelines were inspired by Mozilla’s code of conduct enforcement ladder.
For answers to common questions about this code of conduct, see the FAQ at https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq. Translations are available at https://www.contributor-covenant.org/translations.

title: I Just Became a Citizen of Aotearoa New Zealand
section: Kaikorero: Response to the Mayor’s Welcome
text:
Kaikorero: Response to the Mayor’s Welcome
Before the ceremony, the organizers asked for a volunteer to say a few words in response to the mayor’s welcome. I did not hesitate to raise my hand.
I came up with a short speech on behalf of all the new citizens attending the ceremony. Here, I want to share what becoming a citizen means to me.

title: I Just Became a Citizen of Aotearoa New Zealand
section: “Hei te tau titoki”, the year the titoki tree blooms
text:
“Hei te tau titoki”, the year the titoki tree blooms
This whakatauki speaks of a long-awaited moment, a season of renewal and promise. For me, becoming a citizen of Aotearoa is that moment.
Although I have lived away from my birth country of Mexico for many years, I carry my Mexican whakapapa with pride. Becoming a citizen of Aotearoa does not replace that identity; it enriches it.
Aotearoa has welcomed me through its people, culture, and values. Here, I have embraced te ao Maori, the spirit of whanau, manaakitanga, and kotahitanga. Citizenship is more than a legal status. It is a commitment to contribute, protect, and grow alongside the people of this land.
As the titoki blooms, so does my sense of identity. I am proud of where I come from, and proud now to call myself a citizen of Aotearoa New Zealand.

title: Social Investment in Practice: Lessons from New Zealand’s COVID-19 Data Portal
text: Note: This post reflects my personal views and experiences working on the COVID-19 data portal project. It does not represent the official position of any organisation or government agency.

title: Social Investment in Practice: Lessons from New Zealand’s COVID-19 Data Portal
text:
Introduction
The COVID-19 pandemic did not just challenge public health systems; it reshaped how governments think about investing in people. For those of us working with data, it became a real-world test of how evidence can guide policy under pressure.

title: Social Investment in Practice: Lessons from New Zealand’s COVID-19 Data Portal
section: COVID-19 as a Social Investment Case Study
text:
COVID-19 as a Social Investment Case Study
When COVID-19 arrived, existing policy frameworks were stretched. Decisions needed to be made quickly while still grounded in evidence. That required:

Real-time data to track fast-changing conditions
Integrated analysis across health, social, and economic domains
Predictive models to anticipate downstream impacts
Rapid evaluation of interventions

title: Social Investment in Practice: Lessons from New Zealand’s COVID-19 Data Portal
text:
The Data Foundation
The analytical framework relied on multiple, complementary data sources:

Administrative data: employment, benefit payments, business activity
Survey data: wellbeing and social cohesion
Economic indicators: transactions, productivity, GDP trends
Health data: case counts, testing rates, hospital capacity

title: Social Investment in Practice: Lessons from New Zealand’s COVID-19 Data Portal
text:
Key Insights

1. Data Integration Is Transformative
Linking datasets across agencies revealed patterns no single dataset could capture and improved holistic policy evaluation.


2. Balancing Speed and Rigor
Crisis response demanded iterative analysis: rapid prototypes for immediate decisions, followed by method refinement as data quality improved.


3. Engagement Enables Impact
Data analysis alone does not change policy. Collaboration with policy teams and clear communication formats (dashboards, summaries, interactive tools) made evidence usable.

title: Social Investment in Practice: Lessons from New Zealand’s COVID-19 Data Portal
section: Te Ao Maori Perspectives in Social Investment
text:
Te Ao Maori Perspectives in Social Investment
Social investment aligns with Te Ao Maori principles of collective wellbeing, long-term stewardship, and intergenerational equity. This perspective reminds us that data carries values and responsibilities, not just metrics.

title: Social Investment in Practice: Lessons from New Zealand’s COVID-19 Data Portal
text:
Conclusion
Social investment is about using evidence to improve lives. The pandemic showed both its potential and its complexity at scale. The most important lesson for me was that robust analysis can support empathy and equity, not only efficiency.

title: 5 Critical Lessons from My First Year as a Data Scientist
text: My first year as a professional data scientist was a whirlwind of learning, challenges, and growth. Here are the five most important lessons I wish someone had told me earlier.

title: 5 Critical Lessons from My First Year as a Data Scientist
section: 1. Communication Is More Important Than Code
text:
1. Communication Is More Important Than Code
A perfectly optimized model that stakeholders do not understand or trust will never make it to production.
What I learned:

Start with the business problem, not the algorithm.
Use visualizations to tell a story, not just display data.
Tailor your message to your audience.
Document everything for your future self.

title: 5 Critical Lessons from My First Year as a Data Scientist
section: 2. Clean Data Is a Myth (And That Is Okay)
text:
2. Clean Data Is a Myth (And That Is Okay)
In production, data is messy, incomplete, and sometimes wrong.
Common real-world issues I faced:



Data Issue
Frequency (%)




Missing values
85


Inconsistent formats
72


Duplicates
58


Outliers
45


Schema changes
38



Key takeaway: spend more effort on data pipeline quality and validation than on endless hyperparameter tuning.

title: 5 Critical Lessons from My First Year as a Data Scientist
text:
3. Simple Models Often Win
Simple, interpretable models often outperform complex pipelines in business settings because they are easier to trust and maintain.



Model Complexity
Accuracy (%)
Maintainability (%)




1
82
95


2
87
85


3
91
70


4
93
50


5
94
30



My approach now: start simple, measure impact, then add complexity only when justified.

title: 5 Critical Lessons from My First Year as a Data Scientist
text:
4. Version Control Everything (Not Just Code)
Version control should include data schemas, model artifacts, experiment metadata, and environment configurations.
One of my hardest lessons was being unable to reproduce a model from three months earlier because I did not track the exact data version.

title: 5 Critical Lessons from My First Year as a Data Scientist
section: 5. The Best Model Is the One That Ships
text:
5. The Best Model Is the One That Ships
Perfect is the enemy of done.
Planned timeline:

2 weeks data prep
2 weeks modeling
1 week deployment

Reality:

4 weeks data prep
3 weeks modeling
4 weeks deployment plus ongoing fixes

What helped me ship faster:

Set up CI/CD early.
Start with a minimum viable model.
Iterate with real user feedback.
Automate testing and monitoring from day one.

title: 5 Critical Lessons from My First Year as a Data Scientist
section: Bonus Lesson: Build a Support Network
text:
Bonus Lesson: Build a Support Network
Meetups, online communities, open-source contributions, and writing all helped me grow faster and stay motivated.

title: 5 Critical Lessons from My First Year as a Data Scientist
text:
Conclusion
Technical skills matter, but understanding the full delivery path from business context to production constraints is what creates value.

title: Water Consents Dashboard
text: Northland consent records in this dataset are dominated by irrigation and drinking-water uses, with a clear split between surface water and groundwater sources.

title: Water Consents Dashboard
text: Northland consent records in this dataset are dominated by irrigation and drinking-water uses, with a clear split between surface water and groundwater sources.

title: Water Consents Dashboard
text:
Why this matters for councils
Consented take patterns help identify where pressure on freshwater allocation may increase and where review of expiry profiles, monitoring, or demand-management settings may be most useful.

title: Water Consents Dashboard
text:
Interactive overview


Code
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(plotly)
library(sf)
library(leaflet)

consents <- read_csv("../data/consented-freshwater-takes/take_frame.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

by_use <- consents |>
  count(primaryuse3, sort = TRUE)

plot_ly(
  by_use,
  x = ~reorder(primaryuse3, n),
  y = ~n,
  type = "bar",
  marker = list(color = "#2b7a78")
) |>
  layout(
    xaxis = list(title = "Primary use"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Number of consents"),
    margin = list(b = 120)
  )








Code
map_df <- consents |>
  filter(!is.na(easting_nztm), !is.na(northing_nztm)) |>
  mutate(maxannual = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(maxannual)))

pts <- st_as_sf(
  map_df,
  coords = c("northing_nztm", "easting_nztm"),
  crs = 2193,
  remove = FALSE
) |>
  st_transform(4326)

leaflet(pts) |>
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) |>
  addCircleMarkers(
    radius = 4,
    stroke = FALSE,
    fillOpacity = 0.65,
    color = "#2b7a78",
    popup = ~paste0(
      "Consent: ", resourceconsentid,
      "<br>Use: ", primaryuse3,
      "<br>Source: ", primarysource,
      "<br>Max annual: ", maxannual
    )
  )

title: Groundwater Quality State
text: Groundwater quality reporting tracks multiple contaminant indicators over long time horizons, supporting risk screening for drinking-water safety and ecosystem effects.

title: Groundwater Quality State
text: Groundwater quality reporting tracks multiple contaminant indicators over long time horizons, supporting risk screening for drinking-water safety and ecosystem effects.

title: Groundwater Quality State
text:
Why this matters for councils
Long-term groundwater indicators help identify where sustained pressure may require tighter management, targeted mitigation, or intensified monitoring.

title: Groundwater Quality State
text:
Interactive overview


Code
library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(plotly)
library(leaflet)

gpkg_path <- "../data/mfe-groundwater/groundwater-quality-state-20102014.gpkg"

layers <- st_layers(gpkg_path)
gw <- st_read(gpkg_path, layer = layers$name[1], quiet = TRUE)

# convert Results columns to numeric type
gw_nodg <- st_drop_geometry(gw)

# create numeric result column from likely field names
candidate_result_names <- c("Result", "result", "Value", "median", "c_median", "ann_max", "q_50", "q_25")
present_result_names <- intersect(candidate_result_names, names(gw_nodg))
if (length(present_result_names) == 0) {
  stop("No candidate result columns found in data; available names: ", paste(names(gw_nodg), collapse = ", "))
}

# build Result_char by coalescing over present columns
gw_nodg$Result_char <- NA_character_
for (nm in present_result_names) {
  gw_nodg$Result_char <- dplyr::coalesce(gw_nodg$Result_char, as.character(gw_nodg[[nm]]))
}
gw_nodg$Result_num <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(gsub(",", "", gw_nodg$Result_char)))

# detect indicator column name (try a few common variants)
possible_indicator_names <- c(
  "Aggregated_Back_calculated_parameter",
  "aggregated_back_calculated_parameter",
  "Aggregated_Parameter",
  "parameter",
  "Indicator",
  "aggregated_parameter",
  # common abbreviated indicator columns in this dataset
  "np_id",
  "np_id_name",
  "np_id_units"
)
# prefer descriptive `np_id_name` if present, else first match
if ("np_id_name" %in% names(gw_nodg)) {
  indicator_col <- "np_id_name"
} else {
  indicator_col <- intersect(possible_indicator_names, names(gw_nodg))[1]
}
if (is.na(indicator_col) || is.null(indicator_col)) {
  stop("Indicator column not found in data; available names: ", paste(names(gw_nodg), collapse = ", "))
}

quality_profile <- gw_nodg |>
  group_by(.data[[indicator_col]]) |>
  summarise(records = sum(!is.na(Result_num)), .groups = "drop") |>
  rename(indicator = .data[[indicator_col]]) |>
  arrange(desc(records)) |>
  slice_head(n = 12)

plot_ly(
  quality_profile,
  x = ~reorder(indicator, records),
  y = ~records,
  type = "bar",
  marker = list(color = "#2b7a78")
) |>
  layout(
    xaxis = list(title = "Indicator field"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Non-missing records"),
    margin = list(b = 180)
  )








Code
# transform dataframe into sf
## Coerce coordinate columns (support lowercase names from updated dataset)
gw <- gw %>%
  mutate(
    Easting = coalesce(as.numeric(easting), as.numeric(easting)),
    Northing = coalesce(as.numeric(northing), as.numeric(northing)),
    long = coalesce(as.numeric(long), as.numeric(long), as.numeric(long)),
    lat = coalesce(as.numeric(lat), as.numeric(lat), as.numeric(lat))
  )

## Build sf either from lon/lat
gw_wgs84 <- gw %>%
  filter(!is.na(long) & !is.na(lat)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("long", "lat"), crs = 4326, remove = FALSE)


leaflet(gw_wgs84) |>
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) |>
  addCircleMarkers(
    radius = 4,
    stroke = FALSE,
    fillOpacity = 0.55,
    color = "#ff7a59",
    popup = ~paste0(
      "location: ", s_id,
      "<br>period: ", period,
      "<br>indicator: ", np_id,
      "<br>median: ", median
    ))

text: River nitrogen trend monitoring shows variable trajectories across sites and assessment windows, with trend direction and coverage depending on parameter and period.

title: River Water Quality Nitrogen Trends
text:
Why this matters for councils
River nitrogen trend indicators help target catchments where deteriorating water quality may require tighter source controls, restoration investment, or more focused monitoring.

title: About
text:
I am an environmental scientist with a Doctoral Degree in Biological Sciences and practical experience across field sampling, laboratory workflows and environmental data products. My roles at NIWA and Tatauranga Aotearoa (Stats NZ) combined hands-on sampling protocol development, quality-control of observational networks, and delivery of national data portals and reporting tools that support evidence-based decision-making.
I bring strong technical skills in R and Python for data analysis and visualization, experience drafting technical reports and SOPs, and clear communication with iwi, stakeholders and landowners. Based in the Wairarapa, I am committed to applying my field and data expertise to groundwater monitoring, consenting support and regional planning in the Masterton area.

title: About
text:
The Story Behind Penguinops
Before the world collectively discovered the joys of Zoom fatigue, I was a Marine Biologist. My days were spent pondering the mysteries of the ocean, wrangling algae, and occasionally wondering if my thesis would ever see the light of day. Then, the COVID-19 pandemic arrived, and so my career turned upside down.
During my PhD, my now wife (then my best friend) observed my uncanny resemblance to a Gentoo Penguin. Whether it was my waddling gait after a long day in the lab or my inexplicable fondness for collecting pebbles, she declared the penguin my spirit animal.
Since then, the penguin has become my personal brand. At work, I am known for data science; outside work, I am Pinguino (Spanish for Penguin). Thus, Penguinops was born: a tribute to my aquatic past, my dry sense of humor, and my ongoing quest to bring a little penguin magic to the world of data.

title: About
text:
Work Experience

Kaitatari Hoahoa - Design Analyst

Technical lead for national data science initiatives focused on health and wellbeing data.

Climate Database Technician

Managed and enhanced climate data systems for research and commercial use.
Built an R Shiny app to explore weather station catalogues, error logs, and upload status.
Improved quality control workflows for New Zealand’s manual observer weather station network.
Supported data integrity and operational reliability for climate and environmental monitoring systems.



Molecular Biologist (Part-time)
National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA) | February 2021 - April 2022

Conducted genetic sequencing and analysis for biodiversity and ecology projects.
Produced hundreds of genetic sequences for the marine algae biodiversity programme.
Worked at the intersection of lab science and data analysis for environmental research programmes.
